content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 307 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
March 24, 1977

Honorable Michael Gage
The State Assembly
State Capitol
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Mr. Gage:

Thank you for your letter of February 10, 1977,
seeking clarification with respect to the potential reporting
obligations that could ensue if certain entities provide you
office space in order to make it easier for your constituents
to contact you. You have not indicated that any of the
entities involved employ lobbyists and, hence, we assume for
purposes of this letter that no reporting obligations will
exist under Chapter 6 of the Political Reform Act by reason
of the activities you describe. We also note that because
the purpose for which you will use the office space relates
to your status as an officeholder, the receipt of the office
space free of charge will not create any Chapter 7 (personal
financial disclosure) reporting obligations for you. To the
contrary, any potential reporting obligations would be determined by Chapter 4 (campaign disclosure). See Opinion requested by Willie Brown, 1 FPPC Ops. 67 (No. 75-055, July 2,
1975); Opinion requested by Janet Gray Hayes, 1 FPPC Ops.
210 (No. 75-145, December 4, 1975).

If private entities, whether they are nonprofit or
profit-making, provide the office space for the purpose
outlined in your letter, this will be a contribution to you
within the meaning of Government Code Section §20105 and 2
Cal. Adm. Code section 18215(b)(1). This is because anything
of value received by a candidate for which full and adequate
consideration is not paid is a contribution, unless it is
clear from surrounding circumstances that the payment was
made for personal purposes unrelated to his candidacy or
status as an officeholder." 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18215(b)(1).
Since we assume you are a candidate within the meaning of
the Act—1 and the office space will be provided to you in

1/ The definition of "candidate" includes an individual
"who receives a contribution or makes an expenditure or
gives his consent for any other person to receive a contribution
or make an expenditure with a view to bringing about his
nomination or election to any elective office...." Gov.
Code §82007.

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 · EXECUTIVE LEGAL 322-5901 · ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

Honorable Michael Gage
March 24, 1977
Page Two


connection with your status as an officeholder, providing
the office space will constitute a contribution. The value
of the contribution will be the amount that it would normally
cost to rent the space for the period of time you will use
it.

     On the other hand, if the office space is provided
by a public entity, such as a city or a county, we do not
think that under the circumstances outlined in your letter
this would constitute a contribution. The Commission has
indicated that in order to determine whether payments received
by a candidate from a governmental entity are contributions
it is necessary to distinguish between those payments which
provide a political benefit and those which provide a benefit
related primarily to the recipient's duties and responsibilities
as an officeholder. If the benefit falls into the latter
category and is not "political," in the sense that it is
directly related to seeking nomination or election to an
elective office, the payment is not a contribution. We
think that when a public entity provides you office space to
enable your constituents to contact you, and the space is in
fact used for that purpose, the benefit involved is primarily
related to your duties and responsibilities as an officeholder
and, hence, no contribution is received by you.

     I hope the foregoing guidelines are responsive to
your questions. If you have any further questions, please
do not hesitate to contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director

SANTA ROSA 95404

□ 300 TUOLUMNE STREET
SUITE B
VALLEJO 94590
(707) 642-4433

177003

COMMITTEES
REVENUE AND TAXATION
LABOR, EMPLOYMENT AND
CONSUMER AFFAIRS
JOINT COMMITTEE ON FAIRS
ALLOCATION AND
CLASSIFICATION
VICE CHAIRMAN
ELECTIONS AND
REAPPORTIONMENT

Assembly

California Legislature

MICHAEL GAGE
ASSEMBLYMAN, EIGHTH DISTRICT
REPRESENTING NAPA, SOLANO, AND SONOMA COUNTIES

February 10, 1977

Fair Political Practices Commission
925 J Street, Suite 522
Sacramento, California    95314

Dear Commissioners:

Please help me clarify the following situation:

Facts

1. As a legislator from a rural area, I represent three counties,
   seven unincorporated cities and at least one densely populated
   unincorporated area.

2. My state office allowance is $435.00. I have established two
   offices with this allotment (Vallejo and Santa Rosa).

3. In an effort to make the services of my office accessible to
   more people throughout the Eighth Assembly District, I would
   like to establish office hours on a regular basis in other
   communities within the district.

4. I have arranged for office hours (four hours per week) to be
   held at the Napa Senior Center in an office under a City of
   Napa contract to the Napa County Office on Aging (a nonprofit
   organization).

   I have also established office hours (four hours every other
   week) in the Veterans' Memorial Building in the City of
   Sonoma.

   I am discussing with city officials in the communities of
   Yountville, St. Helena and Calistoga, the feasibility of
   establishing office hours in their council chambers one
   day a month (one and one-half hours each).

Fair Political Practices Commission
February 10, 1977
Page Two


Questions

Are there reporting requirements for the parties involved in
these arrangements?

If so, why?

If so, how?

Finally, are there different requirements if the donated space
is from cities and counties; nonprofit organizations; or private-
for-profit firms?

Comment

Please recognize that there is little likelihood of a public
entity providing this service to their constituents if they
must report a gift or payment to a legislator or his/her campaign.

Sincerely,

[signature]
MIKE GAGE

sections.question:

If so, why?

If so, how?

Comment

Please recognize that there is little likelihood of a public entity providing this service to their constituents if they must report a gift or payment to a legislator or his/her campaign.
sections.facts:
1. As a legislator from a rural area, I represent three counties, seven unincorporated cities and at least one densely populated unincorporated area.

2. My state office allowance is $435.00. I have established two offices with this allotment (Vallejo and Santa Rosa).

3. In an effort to make the services of my office accessible to more people throughout the Eighth Assembly District, I would like to establish office hours on a regular basis in other communities within the district.

4. I have arranged for office hours (four hours per week) to be held at the Napa Senior Center in an office under a City of Napa contract to the Napa County Office on Aging (a nonprofit organization).

I have also established office hours (four hours every other week) in the Veterans' Memorial Building in the City of Sonoma.

I am discussing with city officials in the communities of Yountville, St. Helena and Calistoga, the feasibility of establishing office hours in their council chambers one day a month (one and one-half hours each).

Fair Political Practices Commission  
February 10, 1977  
Page Two
